items.question_text: What is the minimum distance outside the approach gate at which arriving aircraft must be vectored to intercept the final approach course?
items.explanation: 5-9-1a requires vectors to intercept the final approach course at least 2 miles outside the approach gate unless one of the listed exceptions applies.

choices.text: At least 2 miles outside the approach gate
At least 5 miles outside the approach gate
At least 3 miles outside the approach gate
At least 1 mile outside the approach gate

items.question_text: When may an aircraft be vectored to intercept the final approach course closer than 2 miles outside the approach gate but no closer than the approach gate?
items.explanation: 5-9-1a1 permits vectors closer than 2 miles (but not closer than the approach gate) when the reported ceiling is at least 500 feet above the MVA/MIA and visibility is at least 3 miles.

choices.text: When the reported ceiling is at least 500 feet above the MVA/MIA and visibility is at least 3 miles
When the pilot requests a visual approach
When the controller deems it necessary for spacing

items.question_text: If specifically requested by the pilot, how close to the final approach fix may an aircraft be vectored to intercept the final approach course?
items.explanation: 5-9-1a2 permits vectors inside the approach gate but no closer than the final approach fix when specifically requested by the pilot.

choices.text: Inside the approach gate but no closer than the final approach fix
1 mile inside the final approach fix

items.question_text: What vertical separation must be provided between aircraft on opposite base legs unless another form of approved separation is established during turn-on to final approach?
items.explanation: 5-9-1b requires a minimum of 1,000 feet vertical separation between aircraft on opposite base legs.

choices.text: A minimum of 3 miles radar separation

items.question_text: For a precision approach, at what altitude should the aircraft be when vectored to intercept the final approach course?
items.explanation: 5-9-1c requires the aircraft be at an altitude not above the glideslope/glidepath or below the minimum glideslope intercept altitude.

choices.text: Not above the glideslope/glidepath or below the minimum glideslope intercept altitude
At the altitude published on the approach chart

items.question_text: EN ROUTE. What must be displayed on the radar scope before an aircraft may be vectored to the final approach course?
items.explanation: 5-9-1e1 requires the approach gate and a line depicting the final approach course extending away from the airport be displayed on the radar scope.

choices.text: The approach gate and a line depicting the final approach course extending away from the airport

items.question_text: EN ROUTE. For a precision approach, how far must the final approach course line extend from the approach gate?
items.explanation: 5-9-1e1 requires the line length to extend at least the maximum range of the localizer for a precision approach.

choices.text: At least the maximum range of the localizer

items.question_text: According to TBL 5-9-1, what is the maximum interception angle when the distance from interception point to approach gate is less than 2 miles or triple simultaneous approaches are in use?
items.explanation: TBL 5-9-1 specifies 20 degrees maximum when less than 2 miles from the approach gate or triple simultaneous approaches are in use.

items.question_text: According to TBL 5-9-1, what is the maximum interception angle when the distance from interception point to approach gate is 2 miles or more?
items.explanation: TBL 5-9-1 specifies 30 degrees (45 degrees for helicopters) when the distance is 2 miles or more.

items.question_text: If deviations from the final approach course are observed after initial course interception and the aircraft is OUTSIDE the approach gate, what should the controller do?
items.explanation: 5-9-2b1 requires applying interception angle procedures and, if necessary, vectoring the aircraft for another approach.

choices.text: Apply procedures per interception angles and, if necessary, vector for another approach
Inform the pilot and ask intentions

items.question_text: If deviations from the final approach course are observed after initial course interception and the aircraft is INSIDE the approach gate, what should the controller do?
items.explanation: 5-9-2b2 requires informing the pilot of the aircraft's position and asking intentions.

choices.text: Inform the pilot of the aircraft's position and ask intentions
Vector the aircraft for another approach

items.question_text: EN ROUTE. When using a radar scope range above 125 NM, what must the controller do regarding the final approach course?
items.explanation: 5-9-2c requires the controller to solicit and receive a pilot report that the aircraft is established on the final approach course.

choices.text: Solicit and receive a pilot report that the aircraft is established on the final approach course
The aircraft cannot be vectored to final
Transfer the aircraft to approach control

items.question_text: When should the controller inform the aircraft that a vector will take it across the final approach course?
items.explanation: 5-9-3 requires informing the aircraft whenever a vector will take it across the final approach course and stating the reason.

choices.text: Whenever a vector will take it across the final approach course
Only when the aircraft requests it

items.question_text: What is the purpose of informing an aircraft when a vector will take it across the final approach course?
items.explanation: The note to 5-9-3 explains the pilot is not expected to turn inbound on the final approach course unless approach clearance has been issued.

choices.text: So the pilot is not expected to turn inbound unless approach clearance has been issued
To ensure the pilot maintains altitude
To initiate a missed approach procedure

items.question_text: What must be issued to an aircraft before it reaches the approach gate?
items.explanation: 5-9-4 requires issuing position relative to a fix, vector to intercept final if required, and approach clearance (except for radar approaches).

choices.text: Position relative to a fix, vector to intercept if required, and approach clearance (except radar approaches)

items.question_text: When may approach clearance be issued?
items.explanation: 5-9-4c permits approach clearance only after the aircraft is established on a segment of a published route or IAP, or assigned an altitude to maintain until established.

choices.text: After established on a published route/IAP segment or assigned altitude until established
At any time the controller deems appropriate
Only when the pilot requests it
Only after the aircraft is inside the approach gate

items.question_text: If no fix is portrayed on the radar display or prescribed in the procedure on the final approach course, what position information should be issued?
items.explanation: 5-9-4a requires position relative to the navigation aid providing final approach guidance or relative to the airport.

choices.text: Position relative to the navigation aid providing final approach guidance or relative to the airport
Position relative to the nearest VOR
Position relative to the outer marker

items.question_text: During simultaneous independent parallel approaches, the primary responsibility for navigation on the final approach course rests with whom?
items.explanation: 5-9-5b5 states the primary responsibility for navigation on the final approach course rests with the pilot.

items.question_text: When conducting simultaneous independent parallel approaches, what separation must be provided between aircraft during turn-on?
items.explanation: 5-9-5 requires a minimum of 1,000 feet vertical or 3 miles radar separation between aircraft during turn-on to parallel final.

choices.text: 1,000 feet vertical or 3 miles radar separation
2,000 feet vertical or 5 miles radar separation
500 feet vertical or 2 miles radar separation
1,000 feet vertical or 5 miles radar separation

items.question_text: What does 'EoR' stand for in the context of radar approaches?
items.explanation: EoR stands for Established on RNP, referring to an aircraft established on an initial or intermediate segment of an RNAV approach.

items.question_text: During simultaneous dependent approaches, what separation must be provided between aircraft on adjacent final approach courses?
items.explanation: 5-9-6 requires a minimum of 1.5 miles radar separation between aircraft on adjacent final approach courses.

choices.text: A minimum of 1.5 miles radar separation
A minimum of 3 miles radar separation
A minimum of 1,000 feet vertical separation

items.question_text: What is the minimum runway centerline separation for simultaneous independent close parallel approaches with a field elevation at or below 5,000 feet MSL?
items.explanation: 5-9-7 requires runway centerlines separated by at least 4,300 feet but no more than 9,000 feet when field elevation is at or below 5,000 feet MSL.

choices.text: At least 4,300 feet but no more than 9,000 feet

items.question_text: For PRM approaches, what additional frequency is used besides the local control frequency?
items.explanation: 5-9-7d6 specifies a transmit-only secondary PRM frequency is used in addition to the local control frequency.

items.question_text: When must communications transfer to the tower be completed during simultaneous parallel approaches?
items.explanation: 5-9-7d7 requires communications transfer to tower to be completed prior to losing 1,000 feet vertical or 3 miles radar separation between aircraft.

choices.text: Prior to losing 1,000 feet vertical or 3 miles radar separation
When the aircraft is on final

items.question_text: When is radar monitoring terminated during simultaneous parallel approaches?
items.explanation: 5-9-7e4 lists: visual separation applied, aircraft reports approach lights/runway in sight, or aircraft is 1 mile or less from runway threshold.

choices.text: When visual separation is applied, aircraft reports approach lights/runway in sight, or is within 1 mile of threshold
When the aircraft is on the ground
When the tower accepts the aircraft

items.question_text: When must PRM approaches be assigned?
items.explanation: 5-9-8b requires PRM approaches when conducting instrument approaches to dual and triple parallel runways with centerlines separated by less than 4,300 feet.

choices.text: When conducting approaches to dual/triple parallel runways with centerlines less than 4,300 feet apart

items.question_text: What is SOIA?
items.explanation: SOIA stands for Simultaneous Offset Instrument Approaches.

items.question_text: What is the required runway centerline separation for SOIA operations?
items.explanation: 5-9-9a requires parallel runways with centerlines separated by at least 750 feet and less than 3,000 feet.

choices.text: At least 750 feet and less than 3,000 feet

items.question_text: For runways less than 2,500 feet apart during SOIA, when must wake vortex spacing be applied between aircraft on adjacent final approach courses?
items.explanation: 5-9-9g3 requires wake vortex spacing per 5-5-4 when the ceiling is less than 500 feet above the MVA for runways less than 2,500 feet apart.

choices.text: When the ceiling is less than 500 feet above the MVA
At all times regardless of weather
When the ceiling is greater than 500 feet above the MVA

items.question_text: What is the minimum runway centerline separation for simultaneous independent approaches to widely-spaced parallel runways with field elevation at or below 5,000 feet MSL?
items.explanation: 5-9-10b requires runway centerlines separated by more than 9,000 feet for field elevation at or below 5,000 feet MSL.

items.question_text: During widely-spaced parallel approaches, when an aircraft deviates toward the adjacent approach course, what is the first action?
items.explanation: 5-9-10d1 requires instructing the aircraft to return to the correct final approach course immediately.

choices.text: Instruct the aircraft to return to the correct final approach course immediately

items.question_text: What must be done when an aircraft executing a simultaneous approach goes around prior to losing approved reduced separation?
items.explanation: 5-9-11 requires expeditiously issuing control instructions to increase separation and establish approved separation, plus wake turbulence advisories per 2-1-20.

choices.text: Expeditiously issue control instructions to increase separation and establish approved separation
The other aircraft must also go around
No action required if separation still exists
Only wake turbulence advisories must be issued

items.situation: An arriving aircraft is 3 miles from the approach gate. The reported ceiling is 600 feet above the MVA and visibility is 5 miles.
items.question_text: Can you vector this aircraft to intercept the final approach course closer than 2 miles outside the approach gate?

choices.text: Yes, because ceiling is at least 500 ft above MVA/MIA and visibility is at least 3 miles. Vector no closer than the approach gate.
No, the minimum is always 2 miles outside the approach gate.
Yes, vector to intercept at the final approach fix.
Only if the pilot specifically requests it.

items.explanation: 5-9-1A1 permits vectors closer than 2 miles when ceiling is at least 500 ft above MVA/MIA and visibility is at least 3 miles.

items.instruction: What is the maximum interception angle you may assign?
items.situation: An arriving aircraft is 3 miles from the approach gate and you need to vector it to intercept the final approach course. It is not a helicopter.
items.question_text: According to TBL 5-9-1, what is the maximum interception angle?
items.explanation: With distance 2 miles or more from the approach gate, the maximum interception angle is 30 degrees (45 for helicopters).

items.instruction: What must you tell the pilot and what phraseology do you use?
items.situation: You need to vector an arriving aircraft across the final approach course for spacing.
items.question_text: What is the correct action?

choices.text: Inform the aircraft and state the reason. Say: EXPECT VECTORS ACROSS FINAL FOR SPACING.
Just issue the vector without informing the pilot.
Issue the vector and tell the pilot to turn inbound at their discretion.

items.explanation: 5-9-3 Requires informing the aircraft whenever a vector will take it across the final approach course and stating the reason.

items.situation: You are conducting simultaneous independent parallel ILS approaches with an NTZ. An aircraft on one final approach course begins to deviate toward the adjacent approach course and in your judgment will penetrate the NTZ.
items.question_text: What actions must you take?

choices.text: Instruct the deviating aircraft to return to course AND instruct the adjacent aircraft to alter course to avoid
Only instruct the deviating aircraft to return to course
Issue missed approaches to both aircraft
Wait for the aircraft to self-correct

items.explanation: 5-9-7E2 and e3 require both: instruct the deviating aircraft to return to course AND instruct the adjacent aircraft to alter course to avoid.

items.instruction: What must you do regarding wake turbulence?
items.situation: During SOIA operations with parallel runways 2,000 feet apart and a ceiling of 300 feet above the MVA, an aircraft is trailing on the offset approach.
items.question_text: What wake turbulence separation applies?

choices.text: Apply wake vortex spacing per 5-5-4 and issue all applicable wake turbulence advisories
Wake vortex spacing is not required because they are on adjacent courses
Only issue wake turbulence advisories, no spacing required

items.explanation: 5-9-9G3 requires wake vortex spacing per 5-5-4 when ceiling is less than 500 ft above MVA and runways are less than 2,500 ft apart. 5-9-9g4 requires all wake turbulence advisories.
